title: Academy
subtitle: Fundamentals every serious player must master, from beginner to advanced.

math.title: Intuitive Math
math.desc: Pot odds, outs counting and call/fold, exercises generated from your own error spots.

board_strength.title: Hand Strength on the Board
board_strength.desc: Classify your hand and board texture. Knowing where you stand is the foundation of every post-flop decision.

position.title: Position Fundamentals
position.desc: Understand why position is the most persistent asset in poker and how it affects your realized equity.

math_intermediate.title: Equity & Rule of 2/4
math_intermediate.desc: Calculate equity with outs, use the 2/4 rule, and combine draws with pot odds without a calculator.

ranges.title: GTO Ranges: Open (RFI)
ranges.desc: Train just the opening decision: fold or raise by position and stack, graded against the solver ranges.

bet_sizing.title: Bet Sizing 101
bet_sizing.desc: Every wrong bet has an invisible cost. Learn the correct sizings and what each one communicates to opponents.

tournament.title: Tournament Context
tournament.desc: SPR & commitment, ICM on the bubble and FT, equity in 3-bet pots, spots where chip EV ≠ tournament EV.

ranges_advanced.title: GTO Ranges: Full
ranges_advanced.desc: Every situation: open, defense (call/3-bet) and vs 3-bet (4-bet/call), across stack depths.

multiway.title: Multiway Pots (3+ players)
multiway.desc: When more players enter the hand, the math flips: bluffs die, value tightens, bets shrink. Lesson with table and examples.

leakTrainer.subtitle: GTO spots aimed at your weak spots. Each spot adapts to how you do.
leakTrainer.empty: We don't have enough leaks yet to build your training. Import a few tournaments and come back, the trainer targets exactly where you lose EV.

multiway.title: Pots with 3+ players: why everything changes
multiway.subtitle: When more players enter the hand, the math flips. Here's what to do, with examples on the table.
multiway.s1_title: One on one is one thing. Three is another.
multiway.s1_p1: Almost all the theory you learn early on assumes a heads-up pot: you against one opponent. The moment a second player enters the hand (a multiway pot), almost everything changes. It isn't just 'a bit harder', it's a different game. The reason is simple: for a play to work against several people, it has to get past all of them, not just one.
multiway.scene1_cap: You and two other players see the flop. Now every decision depends on two opposing hands, not one.
multiway.s2_p1: Bluffing is betting hoping the opponent folds. Against one person you can steal plenty of pots. Against two or three, you need all of them to fold at once, and the chance that at least one has a hand to call goes up fast. The result: in a multiway pot, a pure bluff (nothing hand, no draw) almost never turns a profit. If you can't improve, folding cheaply is usually right.
multiway.s2_callout: The more players in the pot, the less you bluff. With 3 or more opponents, a pure bluff is essentially zero. Save the chips for when you have a made hand or a strong draw.
multiway.s3_title: Tighten your value: only strong hands continue
multiway.s3_p1: The hands that continue also change. That middle pair that would call comfortably against one player becomes a fold against three, because now it has to be ahead of several hands at once. Continue with genuinely strong hands and with draws that make the best hands (so-called nut draws). Drop the so-so hands: multiway, they cost you.
multiway.cards_cap: Suited AK on a flop with two cards of that suit is a draw to the best flush: it continues. A middle pair, fine against one opponent, becomes a fold against several.
multiway.s4_p1: With more players in the pot, you don't need to bet as big. The pot is already larger (more players put chips in) and there's a better chance someone calls, so a smaller bet already does the job of getting value and charging the draws. Betting very big without a near-unbeatable hand is usually a trap: you only get paid when you're behind.
multiway.s4_callout: Without a dream hand, bet smaller multiway. An overbet without the nuts is chasing ghosts: whoever calls has already beaten you.
multiway.s5_title: The middle seat is the worst place
multiway.s5_p1: There's one spot that bleeds chips: being in the middle. Picture someone betting before you while players still act behind you. You're sandwiched: if you call, you can still get raised by someone behind, and you don't know what either of them has. In that seat, play tight. Continue only with hands that can take pressure from both sides, and avoid calling wide out of curiosity.
multiway.scene5_cap: Opponent A has bet, you're in the middle, and Opponent B is still to act. Sandwiched: call with fewer hands than you would in a normal pot.
multiway.s6_title: Where you see this in GrindLab
multiway.s6_p1: Our solver handles one-on-one spots. In pots with 3 or more players it doesn't lock a verdict: the card shows the ≈ Approximation seal with our best estimate, so it doesn't punish a defensible play. In those spots, use the rules from this chapter as your guide.
multiway.t1: Pure bluffs collapse multiway. No draw, fold cheap.
multiway.t2: Continue only with strong hands and nut draws. Drop the marginals.
multiway.t3: Bet smaller without the nuts. A multiway overbet is a trap.
multiway.t4: In the middle seat, play tight: you're sandwiched.
multiway.q1: You have a pure bluff (weak hand, no draw) and there are 3 opponents in the pot. What do you do?
multiway.q1e: For the bluff to work, all three would have to fold at once, which almost never happens. With no draw, fold and save chips.
multiway.q2: Multiway, compared to heads-up, your value bet size without the nuts should be:
multiway.q2e: The pot is already bigger and there are more callers, so a smaller bet gets value and charges the draws without exposing you. An overbet without the nuts only gets paid when you're behind.
multiway.q3: You're in the middle seat (players bet before you and others still act behind). The right posture is:
multiway.q3a: Call wide so you're not run over
multiway.q3c: Raise as a bluff most of the time
multiway.q3e: Sandwiched, you can still get raised by someone behind and you face two unknown hands. Continue only with strong hands; calling wide here bleeds chips.
multiway.next_sub: Take this to Training and see how you play the multiway pots from your own game.
